title: Fluorescence Titering Assay for Lentivirus

before_start: {"blocks":[{"key":"f1cp1","text":"Considerations Before You Start","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":31}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"doeb2","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"fhhst","text":"The health of the the target cell line is critical for obtaining accurate titers.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"cdodb","text":"           - Check the cells for mycoplasma regularly","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":33,"length":10}],"data":[]},{"key":"4tbkc","text":"           - Do not over or under-grow your cells.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"1lckl","text":"           - Thaw a new vial of cells after 20-30 passages.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"erku1","text":"Do not add penicillin/streptomycin to the media.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"fnvl4","text":"Titer will vary between cell lines.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"9cuej","text":"It is not recommended that lentiviral supernatants be subjected to multiple freeze-thaw cycles.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"link","mutability":"","data":{"guid":"5F52377585AE470FAA8966C696A022B1","url":"http://bitesizebio.com/9071/how-pure-is-your-cell-culture-broth-comparing-mycoplasma-detection-kits/"}}}}

steps: Seed 75,000 cells into each well of a 6-well dish.
Dilute 525,000 cells into 14 mL of DMEM complete.
Mix well by pipetting or inverting.
Aliquot 2 mL of cell suspension into each well of the 6-well dish.

If using freshly collected virus, filter through a 0.45 μm polyethersulfone filter to remove cells and debris. Lentiviral titer can decrease during cycles of freeze-thaw. If you are freezing and aliquoting virus, it is recommended that you titer from the frozen stock to account for any loss in titer associated with freeze-thaw.
If using frozen virus, rapidly thaw the lentiviral aliquot at 37 °C by agitating in a warm water or bead bath.

Prepare dilutions of the lentivirus into DMEM complete containing 10 μg/mL polybrene. Note, this protocol was developed using low titer lentiviral vectors (10 5 TU/mL). If you anticipate that your viral stock will be higher titer consider additional dilutions. %s %s
Gently aspirate media from the cells.
Add 1.5 mL of a viral dilution to each well (each well gets one dilution with one well left over).
Count the cells in the remaining well, a cell count is required for calculating the titer.
Gently aspirate media and replace with 1 mL of PBS.

Calculate the percent of fluorescent-positive cells in each well. When calculating titer, only consider wells with less than 40% fluorescent-positive cells. Titering methods assume 1 integration event per cell. When the percentage exceeds 40% you risk counting cells with multiple integration events leading to underestimation of the true titer.
Calculate the transduction units per mL (TU/mL) using either the dilution factors (method 1) or the volume of virus (method 2):

Method 1: TU/mL = (Number of cells transduced x Percent fluorescent x Dilution Factor)/(Transduction Volume in mL) Method 1 example: If the 1:100 well has 25% fluorescent cells and 150,000 cells were originally transduced, then there are (150,000 x 0.25 x 100)/(1.5 mL) = 2.5x10 6 TU/mL

Method 2: TU/mL = (Number of cells transduced x Percent fluorescent)/(Virus volume in mL) Method 2 example: If 15 µL of virus added to 150,000 cells resulted in 25% fluorescent cells, then there are (150,000 x 0.25)/(0.015 mL) = 2.5x10 6 TU/mL
For a more accurate titer, take the average of multiple dilutions.
